questions.text: Khudiram Bose was hanged in 1908 for his role in the Muzaffarpur bombing. What was the intended target, and what actually happened?
options: Target: Lord Curzon; outcome: bomb missed entirely and fell in a river
Target: District Judge Kingsford; outcome: bomb hit the wrong carriage and killed two British women (Mrs. Kennedy and her daughter)
Target: Plague Commissioner Walter Rand; outcome: Rand was killed along with his aide
Target: Viceroy Lord Minto; outcome: Minto escaped; Khudiram was shot at the scene
questions.explanation: On 30 April 1908, Khudiram Bose and Prafulla Chaki threw a bomb at Muzaffarpur, Bihar, intending to kill District Judge D.H. Kingsford — known for handing down severe sentences to political activists. However, the bomb hit the wrong carriage — it killed two British women, Mrs. Kennedy and her daughter (who had borrowed Kingsford's carriage). Prafulla Chaki shot himself to avoid arrest. Khudiram Bose was caught, tried, and hanged on 11 August 1908. He was approximately 18-19 years old. His youth, his courage on the gallows (reportedly smiling, holding a copy of the Gita), and the romantic tragedy of his misdirected bomb made him an instant martyr symbol across India. The Chapekar brothers (option C) had killed Plague Commissioner Rand in 1897 — a different incident.
questions.source: SSC CGL — Khudiram Bose, Muzaffarpur bombing (1908), and the wrong carriage outcome is a standard identification question; the target (Kingsford) vs. actual victims (Mrs. Kennedy) is the specific trap

questions.text: The Ghadar Party was founded in 1913. Which of the following correctly identifies where it was founded and who was its first president?
options: Lahore, India — Lala Har Dayal
London, England — V.D. Savarkar
San Francisco, USA — Sohan Singh Bhakna
Vancouver, Canada — Kartar Singh Sarabha
questions.explanation: The Ghadar Party was founded in San Francisco, USA, in 1913 — among the Punjabi Sikh immigrant community of the western United States and Canada. Its first president was Sohan Singh Bhakna, a Punjabi Sikh farmer from Amritsar district. Lala Har Dayal was the key intellectual organizer who built the movement, but Bhakna was the president. The party published the Ghadar newspaper in multiple Indian languages (Urdu, Punjabi, Hindi, Gujarati, Marathi, Tamil) to reach all Indian immigrants. V.D. Savarkar was associated with India House in London, not the Ghadar Party. Kartar Singh Sarabha was a young Ghadar martyr (hanged 1915), not the founder.
questions.source: SSC CGL — Ghadar Party founding (San Francisco, 1913) with first president (Sohan Singh Bhakna, not Har Dayal) is a frequently tested distinction; location and president are both commonly confused

questions.text: Madanlal Dhingra was hanged in London in 1909. What act had he committed?
options: He attempted to assassinate Lord Curzon at a public ceremony in London
He shot and killed Sir William Hutt Curzon Wyllie (Political ADC to the Secretary of State for India) at a reception in London
He threw a bomb at the British Parliament building in protest against the Morley-Minto Reforms
He assassinated the Secretary of State for India, John Morley, in his office
questions.explanation: Madanlal Dhingra, an India House member deeply influenced by V.D. Savarkar, shot and killed Sir William Hutt Curzon Wyllie — the Political ADC (Aide-de-Camp) to the Secretary of State for India — at a reception of the National Indian Association in London on 1 July 1909. He also shot Dr. Cawas Lalcaca (a Parsi doctor who tried to intervene). Dhingra was arrested immediately, gave a defiant statement in court, and was hanged at Pentonville Prison, London, on 17 August 1909. His act shocked Britain and led to the effective closure of India House. In India, revolutionaries celebrated him as a martyr; Gandhi condemned the act as an act of violence that harmed India's cause.
questions.source: SSC CGL — Madanlal Dhingra and the Curzon Wyllie assassination (London, 1909) is a standard fact; the specific target's identity and the location (London) are tested

questions.text: The Komagata Maru incident (1914) is connected to which revolutionary movement, and what happened at Budge Budge?
options: The Anushilan Samiti organized the Komagata Maru voyage; the ship was sunk by British warships in the Bay of Bengal

The Ghadar Party network was involved; when the ship returned to Calcutta after being refused entry to Canada, British authorities tried to arrest passengers at Budge Budge — 19 passengers were killed in the confrontation
The ship carried arms for the 1857-style revolt; the arms were discovered and all passengers were hanged at Budge Budge
Komagata Maru was a British ship transporting political prisoners to the Andaman Islands; Ghadar activists attempted a rescue at Budge Budge
questions.explanation: The Komagata Maru was a Japanese steamship carrying 376 passengers (mostly Punjabi Sikh immigrants) who tried to enter Canada in 1914. Canadian racial exclusion policies refused them entry; the ship sat in Vancouver harbour for two months then was forced to return. The passengers were connected to or sympathetic to the Ghadar movement. When the ship docked at Budge Budge near Calcutta on 29 September 1914, British authorities tried to detain several passengers; passengers resisted; British troops fired — killing 19 passengers. The incident inflamed Ghadar sentiment across the world and accelerated the returning Ghadar activists' revolutionary plans for the 1915 uprising (which failed after betrayal). Budge Budge and the 19 deaths are the specific testable facts.
questions.source: SSC CGL — Komagata Maru (1914) and the Budge Budge incident (19 killed) is a standard sequential question connecting the ship to the Ghadar movement

questions.text: India House in London was founded by Shyamji Krishna Varma. V.D. Savarkar is often associated with it. What was Savarkar's specific connection and what happened to him?
options: Savarkar founded India House in 1905 and Shyamji was a member; Savarkar was deported to India in 1907 peacefully

Savarkar came to India House on a Shyamji scholarship; wrote his 1857 book there; was arrested in 1910; attempted escape by jumping into the sea at Marseilles; was sent to Andaman Cellular Jail (1911-1921)
Savarkar organized the Dhingra assassination from India House; he was arrested immediately and hanged alongside Dhingra in 1909
Savarkar was a paid British informant at India House; he helped identify revolutionary members and was rewarded with safe passage to India
questions.explanation: Savarkar's India House connection: He arrived in London on a Shyamji Krishna Varma fellowship, stayed at India House, wrote 'The Indian War of Independence 1857' (1909, banned before publication, printed in Netherlands), and was involved in organizing the smuggling of pistols into India (the Nashik Conspiracy Case). He was arrested in London in 1910. The famous Marseilles escape attempt: while being transported by ship under arrest, he jumped into the sea at Marseilles harbour, swam to shore, and appealed to French authorities for asylum. Britain got him back through diplomatic pressure. The Hague Permanent Court of Arbitration was asked to adjudicate — ruled France's handover was valid though irregular. Savarkar was sentenced to 50 years' transportation (two consecutive 25-year terms) and sent to Andaman Cellular Jail in 1911; released 1921; later confined to Ratnagiri until 1937.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated because Savarkar's connection to India House (as member, not founder) and the Marseilles escape attempt are specific detail questions in higher-difficulty papers

questions.text: Rash Behari Bose organized the 1912 Delhi Durbar bomb attack on Viceroy Hardinge. He later played a crucial role in events connected to Subhas Chandra Bose. What was this connection?
options: Rash Behari Bose became Subhas Chandra Bose's political mentor and helped him escape from India in 1941

After escaping to Japan in 1915 and becoming a Japanese citizen, Rash Behari Bose founded the Indian Independence League (1942) and handed its leadership to Subhas Chandra Bose, who built it into the Indian National Army (INA)
Rash Behari Bose and Subhas Chandra Bose were cousins who jointly planned the INA from Singapore
Rash Behari Bose had nothing to do with Subhas Chandra Bose — the connection is a historical myth
questions.explanation: Rash Behari Bose (1886-1945) is the crucial link between Phase 1 and Phase 2 revolutionary nationalism. After organizing the Delhi Durbar bomb attack (1912), he continued revolutionary activities but eventually fled to Japan in 1915 disguised as a Bengali nobleman and scholar. He married a Japanese woman, became a Japanese citizen, became famous in Japan for introducing the Indian curry to Japanese cuisine (founding a curry restaurant chain — still famous). When Japan entered WWII (1941), he founded the Indian Independence League (IIL) in 1942 to organize Indian POWs and civilians in Southeast Asia. Feeling he lacked the charisma for the role, he invited Subhas Chandra Bose to come to Singapore to lead. Subhas took over in 1943, built the IIL into the Indian National Army (INA), and launched the famous 'Chalo Delhi' (March to Delhi) campaign. Rash Behari and Subhas were NOT related — they were connected by revolutionary history, not blood.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated because students often know the INA story (Subhas) without knowing the Rash Behari Bose foundation that preceded it; the Phase 1-Phase 2 revolutionary continuity is tested in integrated questions

questions.text: Kartar Singh Sarabha was 19 years old when he was hanged in 1915. Why is he particularly significant in the history of Indian revolutionary nationalism?
options: He was the youngest person ever to be executed by the British — his age record was never broken

He was the leading organizer of the Ghadar movement's 1915 uprising plan; he returned from USA to India specifically to lead the revolution; he was Bhagat Singh's personal idol — Bhagat Singh carried his photo constantly
He threw the bomb that wounded Viceroy Hardinge at the Delhi Durbar (1912)
He was the first Ghadar member to infiltrate the British Indian Army and was the movement's primary informant on troop dispositions
questions.explanation: Kartar Singh Sarabha (1896-1915) is one of the most romantic figures of revolutionary nationalism. He was a student at the University of California, Berkeley, when he joined the Ghadar movement. When the call came in 1914 to return to India and foment revolution, he responded without hesitation — sailing back despite knowing the risks. He was a key organizer of the planned February 1915 Ghadar uprising; when it was betrayed, he was arrested and hanged at Lahore Central Jail on 16 November 1915 — just 19 years old. His significance: Bhagat Singh (who was born in 1907, so was only 8 when Sarabha died) grew up worshipping him. Bhagat Singh carried Sarabha's photograph in his pocket constantly and called him his guru. The line from Sarabha to Bhagat Singh is the clearest expression of revolutionary tradition passing across generations.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated in SSC notes but the Sarabha-Bhagat Singh connection is specifically tested in questions about the revolutionary tradition's continuity across phases

questions.text: Bhupendranath Dutta co-founded the Yugantar revolutionary newspaper. What is his notable family connection?
options: He was Aurobindo Ghosh's younger brother
He was Rabindranath Tagore's cousin
He was Swami Vivekananda's younger brother
He was Tilak's son-in-law
questions.explanation: Bhupendranath Dutta was the younger brother of Swami Vivekananda (Narendranath Datta). This is a remarkable family connection: while his older brother founded the Ramakrishna Mission advocating spiritual universalism and service, Bhupendranath became a revolutionary nationalist who co-founded Yugantar — one of the most radical revolutionary newspapers of the era. Bhupendranath was later arrested by the British, served prison time, spent years in Germany studying Marxism, and returned to India as a Marxist. The two brothers thus represent two very different responses to colonial India's condition: spiritual universalism vs. revolutionary nationalism. The Barindra Kumar Ghosh - Aurobindo Ghosh sibling connection (both brothers, both revolutionary) is the more famous pairing; the Dutta family connection is a lesser-known but equally interesting crossover.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because Bhupendranath Dutta appears as a Yugantar co-founder but his famous family connection (Vivekananda's brother) is a crossover fact tested in 'who is related to whom' questions

questions.text: The Andaman Islands Cellular Jail was called 'Kala Pani.' Why was this specific term used, and what made it particularly effective as a punishment?
options: The water around the Andaman Islands was literally black due to oil seepage from British ships — prisoners could see it from the jail and it was psychologically disturbing

'Kala Pani' means Black Waters — crossing the sea was considered ritually polluting for upper-caste Hindus (loss of caste); combined with solitary confinement cells designed to prevent communication and brutal forced labour, it was designed to break prisoners physically and spiritually
The jail's water supply came from a black granite aquifer — prisoners were forced to drink this 'black water' as a punishment
Kala Pani referred to the black colour of the uniforms forced on prisoners — degrading their status to that of convicts
questions.explanation: 'Kala Pani' (Black Waters) was the Hindi term for the Andaman Islands penal settlement — it carried multiple layers of punishment: (1) Ritual: For upper-caste Hindus, crossing the sea (samudr laanghan) was considered ritual pollution, causing loss of caste — one returned from Kala Pani as an outcaste; (2) Psychological: The extreme distance from home, family, and community; (3) Physical: The Cellular Jail (built 1906) had 693 individual cells designed to prevent any prisoner-to-prisoner communication — solitary confinement on a massive scale; prisoners could hear but not see each other; (4) Labour: Forced labour — oil pressing, rope-making, breaking rocks — was exhausting and often physically destructive. The combination made Kala Pani not just a prison but a multi-layered system of spiritual, psychological, and physical destruction. Notable prisoners who survived it and returned include Savarkar and Barindra Kumar Ghosh.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because 'Kala Pani' appears as a one-line fact but its significance (ritual pollution aspect + Cellular Jail design) is rarely explained; appears in analytical questions about British penal methods

questions.text: The Ghadar Party published its newspaper in multiple Indian languages. Why was this multi-language approach significant?
options: It was required by US government regulations, which mandated that foreign-language newspapers register in all languages of their readership

The party needed to reach Indian immigrants of different regional and linguistic backgrounds across USA and Canada; it also needed to smuggle the paper into different parts of India where different languages were spoken
Har Dayal was a professor of linguistics and insisted on linguistic diversity as a matter of principle
The multi-language printing was a cover — the real content was in a secret code embedded in the Urdu version only
questions.explanation: The Ghadar newspaper was published in Urdu, Punjabi, Hindi, Gujarati, Marathi, Tamil, and other languages for very practical reasons: (1) The Indian immigrant community in North America was diverse — Punjabi Sikhs (the majority), but also Gujaratis, Marathis, Tamils; a single-language paper would exclude most; (2) For distribution in India, different regions read different languages — a paper smuggled to Maharashtra needed to be in Marathi or Hindi; one going to Tamil Nadu needed Tamil; (3) Maximising reach: the goal was revolution across India, which meant reaching as many Indians as possible regardless of education in a particular language. This multi-language publishing was innovative and showed organisational sophistication. The newspaper became the party's identity — the party was named after the newspaper ('Ghadar' = revolt), not the other way around.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because the Ghadar newspaper's multi-language aspect is a one-line fact but the strategic reasoning (diverse immigrant community + smuggling into different Indian regions) is the testable analytical point

questions.text:
Match the revolutionary figure with their associated act or organization:
1. Khudiram Bose        A. Founded India House, London
2. Shyamji Krishna Varma B. Muzaffarpur bombing (1908)
3. Sohan Singh Bhakna   C. Shot Curzon Wyllie in London (1909)
4. Madanlal Dhingra     D. First President, Ghadar Party
questions.explanation: Correct matches: (1) Khudiram Bose → B. Muzaffarpur bombing, 30 April 1908 (threw bomb targeting Judge Kingsford; wrong carriage; two British women killed; hanged 11 August 1908 aged ~18-19); (2) Shyamji Krishna Varma → A. Founded India House, London (1905; also published 'The Indian Sociologist'); (3) Sohan Singh Bhakna → D. First President of the Ghadar Party (1913, San Francisco; a Punjabi Sikh farmer from Amritsar); (4) Madanlal Dhingra → C. Shot Curzon Wyllie in London (1 July 1909; at National Indian Association reception; hanged 17 August 1909). This multi-figure matching is the most frequently tested format for Phase 1 Revolutionary Nationalism in SSC CGL.
questions.source: Revolutionary figure matching (Khudiram/Muzaffarpur, Shyamji/India House, Bhakna/Ghadar President, Dhingra/Curzon Wyllie) is the most frequently tested format for this topic

questions.text: Which of the following statements about the Ghadar Party is INCORRECT?
options: It was founded in San Francisco in 1913
Lala Har Dayal was the key intellectual organizer
Kartar Singh Sarabha, a 19-year-old Ghadar activist, was hanged in 1915
The Ghadar Party was founded in Lahore by Lala Lajpat Rai during the anti-Partition movement
questions.explanation: The INCORRECT statement is option D. The Ghadar Party was NOT founded in Lahore by Lala Lajpat Rai — it was founded in San Francisco, USA, in 1913, primarily by the community of Punjabi Sikh immigrants. Lala Har Dayal was the key intellectual organizer; Sohan Singh Bhakna was the first president. Lala Lajpat Rai was a Congress Extremist in India — he had been deported to Mandalay in 1907 during Punjab unrest (not Andaman), but he had nothing to do with founding the Ghadar Party. All other options are correct: Ghadar was founded in San Francisco 1913 (A); Har Dayal was the organizer (B); Kartar Singh Sarabha was a 19-year-old martyr hanged in 1915 (C). The Lahore confusion is the standard trap — Lahore was where the Lahore Conspiracy Cases were tried and where many Ghadaris were executed, but not where the party was founded.
questions.source: The INCORRECT statement format for Ghadar Party (founding location = San Francisco, NOT Lahore) is frequently tested; the Har Dayal vs. Bhakna vs. Lajpat Rai confusion is the standard trap

questions.text: The Anushilan Samiti was one of the most important revolutionary organizations of Bengal. Which of the following correctly describes it?
options: A prayer society founded in 1902 by Aurobindo Ghosh that advocated peaceful resistance through non-cooperation
A fitness and physical culture organization (1902) that evolved into a revolutionary underground network; connected to Barindra Kumar Ghosh's Maniktala bomb factory and the Alipore Bomb Case
A secret literary society founded by Tagore that published revolutionary poetry disguised as folklore
The political wing of the Brahmo Samaj that organized the anti-Partition movement in Bengal
questions.explanation: The Anushilan Samiti (Anushilan = practice/cultivation) was founded in 1902 in Calcutta — initially as a fitness and physical culture organization (gymnasium, wrestling, physical training). It evolved into a revolutionary underground organization: recruiting idealistic young men, training them physically for armed resistance, and eventually connecting with actual bomb-making and weapons procurement. It split into Calcutta and Dhaka branches; the Dhaka branch was more militantly activist. The organization was connected to Barindra Kumar Ghosh (Aurobindo's brother) who ran the Maniktala bomb factory — leading to the Alipore Bomb Case (1908). Aurobindo Ghosh was associated with revolutionary networks (through his writings and his brother's activities) but did not found the Anushilan Samiti — that was Promotha Mitter and others.
questions.source: Anushilan Samiti identification (1902, Bengal, fitness-turned-revolutionary, connected to Alipore Bomb Case) is a standard organizational identification question in SSC CGL
